instrument.study_title: Delphi Expert Validation for Web3 AI Agent Taxonomy

expertise_areas: AI/ML researchers with agent systems experience
recruitment_criteria: Minimum 3 years experience in relevant domain
Published at least 1 peer-reviewed paper in related area
Active involvement in Web3/blockchain ecosystem preferred

round_1.description: Open-ended exploration: experts independently rate dimensions and categories, classify real-world entities, and compare against existing frameworks.

part_a_dimension_validation.definition: Degree of autonomous decision-making, ranging from NONE (pure deterministic script) through REACTIVE (fixed-rule responses), ADAPTIVE (parameter adjustment based on feedback), PROACTIVE (independent planning and goal pursuit), to COLLABORATIVE (multi-agent coordination).
levels: NONE (Level 0): Pure script, deterministic, no adaptation
REACTIVE (Level 1): Responds to events with fixed rules
ADAPTIVE (Level 2): Adjusts parameters based on environment
PROACTIVE (Level 3): Plans and initiates actions independently
part_a_dimension_validation.literature_basis: Derived from Russell & Norvig (2020) agent architecture hierarchy and Wooldridge & Jennings (1995) four-property framework. Simplified from Parasuraman et al. (2000) 10-level automation to 5 levels due to limited observability through blockchain transaction data.

questions.text: Is the dimension 'Autonomy Level (自主性等级)' necessary for distinguishing AI agents from other automated systems in the Web3 context?
Is the dimension 'Autonomy Level (自主性等级)' sufficient as defined, or are additional sub-dimensions or modifications needed? Please elaborate.
Rate the clarity of the definitions for 'Autonomy Level (自主性等级)' and its levels.
Rate the completeness of the levels within 'Autonomy Level (自主性等级)'. Are all meaningful levels covered?
Are there additional dimensions not captured by our three-dimension framework that are essential for classifying Web3 AI agents? If so, please describe them.

part_a_dimension_validation.definition: Where the agent operates and what data sources it accesses. On-chain Only (fully on-chain smart contract logic), Hybrid/Off-chain-to-On-chain (off-chain compute, on-chain execution), Cross-chain (operates across multiple blockchains), Multi-modal (combines on-chain actions with off-chain data sources such as APIs, LLMs, social media).
levels: ON-CHAIN ONLY: Fully on-chain smart contract execution
CROSS-CHAIN: Operates across multiple blockchain networks
MULTI-MODAL: Combines on-chain + off-chain services (APIs, LLMs)
part_a_dimension_validation.literature_basis: Novel dimension specific to Web3 context. Traditional agent taxonomies do not distinguish environment types at this granularity. Motivated by the unique transparency and constraints of blockchain execution environments.

questions.text: Is the dimension 'Environment Type (环境类型)' necessary for distinguishing AI agents from other automated systems in the Web3 context?
Is the dimension 'Environment Type (环境类型)' sufficient as defined, or are additional sub-dimensions or modifications needed? Please elaborate.
Rate the clarity of the definitions for 'Environment Type (环境类型)' and its levels.
Rate the completeness of the levels within 'Environment Type (环境类型)'. Are all meaningful levels covered?
Are there additional dimensions not captured by our three-dimension framework that are essential for classifying Web3 AI agents? If so, please describe them.

part_a_dimension_validation.definition: The mechanism by which the agent makes decisions. Deterministic (if-then rules), Statistical (ML/probability-based), LLM-driven (large language model reasoning), Reinforcement Learning (policy optimization via reward signals), Hybrid (combination of multiple models).
levels: DETERMINISTIC: If-then rules, fully predictable output
HYBRID: Combination of multiple decision models
part_a_dimension_validation.literature_basis: Extends Russell & Norvig (2020) agent architectures with modern AI decision paradigms (LLM, RL). The distinction between Statistical and LLM-driven is novel and reflects the emergence of foundation-model-based agents.

questions.text: Is the dimension 'Decision Model (决策模型)' necessary for distinguishing AI agents from other automated systems in the Web3 context?
Is the dimension 'Decision Model (决策模型)' sufficient as defined, or are additional sub-dimensions or modifications needed? Please elaborate.
Rate the clarity of the definitions for 'Decision Model (决策模型)' and its levels.
Rate the completeness of the levels within 'Decision Model (决策模型)'. Are all meaningful levels covered?
Are there additional dimensions not captured by our three-dimension framework that are essential for classifying Web3 AI agents? If so, please describe them.

part_b_category_validation.definition: Fully deterministic program that executes a fixed sequence of transactions without any autonomous decision-making.
on_chain_indicators: Fixed gas price or legacy gas pricing

questions.text: Rate how well-defined the category 'Deterministic Script' is.
Rate how distinguishable 'Deterministic Script' is from the other 7 categories.
Rate how representative the provided examples are for 'Deterministic Script'.
Can you provide real-world examples that belong to 'Deterministic Script' that we missed?
Should the category 'Deterministic Script' be modified?
If you selected anything other than 'Keep as-is', please explain your reasoning.

entity.name: jaredfromsubway.eth
entity.description: One of Ethereum's most prolific MEV extractors. Conducts sandwich attacks on DEX trades by monitoring the mempool, front-running victim transactions, and back-running to capture price impact profit. Operates via Flashbots bundles.

questions.text: Which category does 'jaredfromsubway.eth' belong to?
options: Not an agent (Human / Smart Contract / Other)

questions.text: How confident are you in your classification of 'jaredfromsubway.eth'?
Briefly explain your classification rationale for 'jaredfromsubway.eth'.

entity.name: Wintermute
entity.description: A major crypto market maker providing liquidity across centralized and decentralized exchanges. Operates algorithmic trading strategies with high frequency and tight spreads. Interacts primarily with DEX router contracts.

questions.text: Which category does 'Wintermute' belong to?
options: Not an agent (Human / Smart Contract / Other)

questions.text: How confident are you in your classification of 'Wintermute'?
Briefly explain your classification rationale for 'Wintermute'.

entity.name: Autonolas OLAS agent
entity.description: An autonomous agent registered in the Autonolas ServiceRegistry. Manages DeFi positions across multiple protocols (Aave, Uniswap, Yearn), rebalances based on market conditions, and uses a hybrid decision model combining statistical risk assessment with rule-based execution.

questions.text: Which category does 'Autonolas OLAS agent' belong to?

entity.name: Uniswap V2 Router
entity.description: A smart contract that routes token swaps through Uniswap V2 liquidity pools. Executes deterministic swap logic based on the constant product formula. Has no off-chain component or autonomous decision-making.

questions.text: Which category does 'Uniswap V2 Router' belong to?

questions.text: How confident are you in your classification of 'Uniswap V2 Router'?
Briefly explain your classification rationale for 'Uniswap V2 Router'.

part_c_classification_exercise._expected_category: Deterministic Script (or not an agent at all)

entity.name: A personal wallet doing manual trades
entity.description: An externally owned account (EOA) controlled by a human user who manually initiates DEX trades via a frontend (e.g., Uniswap web app). Transactions show circadian rhythm, irregular intervals, and round-number gas prices.

questions.text: Which category does 'A personal wallet doing manual trades' belong to?
options: Not an agent (Human / Smart Contract / Other)

questions.text: How confident are you in your classification of 'A personal wallet doing manual trades'?
Briefly explain your classification rationale for 'A personal wallet doing manual trades'.

entity.name: An Aave liquidation bot
entity.description: A bot that monitors Aave lending positions and triggers liquidation transactions when health factors drop below thresholds. Uses statistical models to estimate gas costs and profitability. Adapts gas bidding strategy based on competition.

questions.text: Which category does 'An Aave liquidation bot' belong to?
options: Not an agent (Human / Smart Contract / Other)

questions.text: How confident are you in your classification of 'An Aave liquidation bot'?
Briefly explain your classification rationale for 'An Aave liquidation bot'.

entity.name: LayerZero relayer
entity.description: A relayer node that monitors source-chain events and submits proof transactions on destination chains. Operates across Ethereum, Arbitrum, Optimism, and Polygon. Deterministic relay logic with adaptive gas pricing.

questions.text: Which category does 'LayerZero relayer' belong to?
options: Not an agent (Human / Smart Contract / Other)

questions.text: How confident are you in your classification of 'LayerZero relayer'?
Briefly explain your classification rationale for 'LayerZero relayer'.

entity.name: AI16Z ELIZA trading agent
entity.description: An agent built on the ELIZA framework that uses GPT-4 to analyze social media sentiment, news, and on-chain data to make trading decisions. Executes token swaps on Uniswap based on LLM reasoning. Posts updates on Twitter.

questions.text: Which category does 'AI16Z ELIZA trading agent' belong to?
options: Not an agent (Human / Smart Contract / Other)

questions.text: How confident are you in your classification of 'AI16Z ELIZA trading agent'?
Briefly explain your classification rationale for 'AI16Z ELIZA trading agent'.

entity.name: A cron job that distributes payroll
entity.description: A cron-scheduled script that runs every two weeks and sends fixed amounts of USDC to a predefined list of employee addresses. No conditional logic, no adaptation, identical calldata every execution.

questions.text: Which category does 'A cron job that distributes payroll' belong to?
options: Not an agent (Human / Smart Contract / Other)

questions.text: How confident are you in your classification of 'A cron job that distributes payroll'?
Briefly explain your classification rationale for 'A cron job that distributes payroll'.

entity.name: A Chainlink oracle node
entity.description: A node operator that submits price feed updates to Chainlink aggregator contracts. Uses deterministic submission logic triggered by price deviation thresholds. Operates within the Chainlink consensus protocol alongside other oracle nodes.

questions.text: Which category does 'A Chainlink oracle node' belong to?
options: Not an agent (Human / Smart Contract / Other)

questions.text: How confident are you in your classification of 'A Chainlink oracle node'?
Briefly explain your classification rationale for 'A Chainlink oracle node'.

part_d_taxonomy_comparison.instructions: Below we present our 8-category taxonomy side-by-side with 5 existing agent classification frameworks. Please review each and answer the following questions.

our_taxonomy.name: Web3 AI Agent Taxonomy (This Paper)
design_principles: Observability: every category distinguishable via on-chain behavior
Mutual exclusivity: unique (autonomy, environment, decision) triple per category
Exhaustiveness: covers all known Web3 agent types

comparison_frameworks.source: Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach, 4th ed.
limitations_for_web3: No distinction for execution environment (on-chain vs off-chain)
Does not account for blockchain-specific constraints
Cannot distinguish MEV searchers from DeFi management agents

comparison_frameworks.source: Is it an Agent, or Just a Program?
strengths: Addresses the 'what is an agent' question directly
limitations_for_web3: Too many dimensions for practical classification
Properties like 'Emotional' and 'Personality' irrelevant to Web3

comparison_frameworks.source: A Model for Types and Levels of Human Interaction with Automation
categories: 10 levels of automation from fully manual to fully automatic,
limitations_for_web3: Designed for human-machine systems, not autonomous software agents
10 levels too granular for blockchain observability
Does not account for multi-agent coordination

comparison_frameworks.source: Guarantees for Autonomy in Cognitive Agent Architecture (Social Commitment Agents)
strengths: Addresses social aspects of agent behavior
Relevant to DAO governance and collective decision-making
limitations_for_web3: Focused on social/normative aspects, not operational classification
Limited applicability to purely technical agent types (MEV, trading)

comparison_frameworks.source: A Roadmap of Agent Research and Development
limitations_for_web3: BDI model hard to observe on-chain
Does not distinguish between blockchain execution environments

questions.text: Rank the following taxonomies from most useful (1) to least useful (6) for classifying AI agents in the Web3/blockchain context.
What does our taxonomy capture that the other frameworks miss? Please be specific.
What do the other frameworks capture that our taxonomy misses? Should we incorporate any of these elements?
Rate the overall applicability of our taxonomy to the Web3 context compared to existing frameworks.
Rate the potential generalizability of our taxonomy beyond Web3 to other AI agent domains.

round_2_template.description: Convergence round: experts see group medians and IQR from Round 1, re-rate items where consensus not reached (IQR > 1), confirm or adjust their classifications.
round_2_template.note: Round 2 instrument is generated dynamically based on Round 1 results. See build_round2_instrument().

round2_instrument.instructions: In Round 1, you and your fellow experts independently rated the taxonomy dimensions, categories, and comparison frameworks. Below we present the group's median ratings and spread (IQR). For items where consensus was NOT reached (IQR > 1), please re-rate considering the group feedback. For items where consensus WAS reached (IQR <= 1), no re-rating is needed unless you have strong objections.
